title: The Complete OlumJS Guide
description: Everything OlumJS in one place — state and reactivity, the full template syntax including transitions, components and props, routing, the global store, lifecycle, scoped CSS, security, and the gotchas — each summarized with a code example, plus a one-screen cheat-sheet to bookmark.

sections.heading: One design principle
sections.body:
OlumJS draws a single line through its whole template syntax: is an attribute fundamentally code or a string? Native HTML already answers it — onclick="" is code, class="" is a string.

- Code-shaped attributes — when, each, key, on*, html — take a JavaScript expression directly inside the quotes.
- Everything else is a literal string, with {expr} for the dynamic bits.

That's the entire mental model. No naked braces, no colon-bindings, no directives to memorize. This guide walks every feature the same way: a sentence, then a snippet.

sections.heading: Component file structure
sections.body: A component is one .html file with up to three parts: a <script> for logic, a scoped <style>, and the template (everything else). State, methods, and props are all in the same closure, so the template can reference them directly. Component tag names are PascalCase — that's how the compiler tells a component from a plain element.
sections.code: Count: {state.count}

sections.heading: State & reactivity
sections.body: Declare reactive state as a literal top-level const state = { … }. Mutating state re-renders the component, and reactivity is deep — nested objects, arrays, Map, and Set are all tracked. Only state is reactive; plain const/let variables are not. Re-renders diff-patch the DOM in place, so untouched nodes — focus, caret, scroll position, a playing video — survive the update, and many writes in the same tick are batched into a single patch.
sections.code: {state.count} — {state.user.name}

sections.heading: Derived values (no "computed")
sections.body: There's no computed keyword — a derived value is just a plain function that reads state. Call it in the template like any other expression; a derived function can call another derived function too.
sections.code: {doubled()} {quadrupled()}

sections.heading: Text interpolation
sections.body: Any {expr} in text is evaluated as JavaScript and HTML-escaped by default, so it's XSS-safe. null and undefined render as an empty string. There is no escape for a literal { — every {…} is treated as interpolation; use {String.fromCharCode(123)} if you need a literal brace.
sections.code:
Hello {state.user.name}

Total: {state.items.length} items

{state.count > 0 ? 'positive' : 'zero'}

sections.heading: Watchers
sections.body: Declare const watcher = { … } with a function per state key. It fires on top-level key assignment only — not a nested mutation — receiving (oldValue, newValue). To make a nested change trigger it, assign a fresh value to the top-level key instead (e.g. spread the object back onto itself).
sections.code:
<script>
  const state = { count: 0, log: "—" };
  const watcher = {
    count(old, next) {
      state.log = `count: ${old} → ${next}`;
    },
  };
</script>

sections.heading: Conditionals: if / else-if / else
sections.body: when is always a JS expression. The element is physically added to or removed from the DOM as the condition changes.
sections.code:
Tab A

Tab B

Fallback

sections.heading: Show / hide
sections.body: Like <if>, but the element stays in the DOM and only its display is toggled. Reach for <show> when you want to preserve DOM state or avoid re-mounting cost; reach for <if> when the element should truly not exist.
sections.code: Stays in the DOM; only display toggles.

sections.heading: Loops: for
sections.body: each is a JS expression with three forms — array (item of list), numeric range (i of N, where i runs 1 → N), and object keys (key in obj). When the loop body is a component, add key so instances are reused by identity across reorders instead of by position. On plain elements, key is a harmless no-op.
sections.code:
• {fruit.name}
    •  Step {i}
    {key}: {state.settings[key]}

sections.heading: Events
sections.body: Native on* attributes hold code, just like real HTML. Use a method call, or an inline arrow that gets extracted into a method automatically. Pass the DOM event with $event. There are no event modifiers — call e.preventDefault() in the handler instead.
sections.code:
<button onclick="inc()">+</button>
<button onclick="inc(), log()">multi</button>
<input oninput="(e) => state.text = e.target.value" />
<input oninput="setValue($event)" />
<form onsubmit="(e) => { e.preventDefault(); save() }"></form>

sections.heading: Two-way binding
sections.body: There is no model attribute — bind manually with a value plus an oninput handler. Explicit, greppable, and it works for any form field.
sections.code: Echo: {state.text}

sections.heading: Attributes: strings vs code
sections.body: String attributes are the default — a literal string with {expr} inside for the dynamic parts, including inline style. Code attributes (when, each, key, on*, html) evaluate their quoted value as JavaScript.
sections.code:
Profile
box
…

sections.heading: Raw HTML (opt-in)
sections.body:
All interpolation is escaped by default. Opt out only for trusted, sanitized HTML, two ways: html="expr" replaces an element's content, and olum.html(value) injects raw HTML inline inside {}. Both are deliberate, greppable bypasses.

Never pass untrusted user content to html= or olum.html() — sanitize first.
sections.code: Intro: {olum.html(state.snippetHtml)} — end.

sections.heading: Components & props
sections.body: Import a child in <script> and use it by its PascalCase tag. A prop is a literal string by default; prop="{expr}" passes the expression's real value and type; and {} inside text yields an interpolated string.
sections.code:
<!-- Parent.html -->
<script>
  import StatusBadge from "./StatusBadge";
  const state = { name: "Ann", score: 5 };
</script>

<StatusBadge
  label="Online"            <!-- "Online"  → string              -->
  count="{state.score}"     <!-- 5         → number (type kept)   -->
  data="{state.user}"       <!-- {...}     → object (type kept)   -->
  greet="Hi {state.name}"   <!-- "Hi Ann"  → interpolated string  -->
/>

sections.heading: Reading props in the child
sections.body: Import props from olum and destructure it once at the top level of <script> — no onMount required. Destructuring is a compiler feature: each destructured name (plus aliases and defaults) compiles into a fresh props().name read, so it stays live after the parent re-renders. The exceptions that only snapshot the initial value: ...rest, nested patterns, computed keys, and destructuring done anywhere other than the top level. Props are read-only — a child requests a change through a callback prop, it never assigns to one.
sections.code: {label}

sections.heading: Slots
sections.body: Content placed between a component's tags is exposed as children on props() — injected as raw, trusted markup (not escaped), so don't route untrusted strings through a slot. Like any other prop, a destructured children stays live.
sections.code:
passed from the parent {children}
no content

sections.heading: Scope: public / private
sections.body: A component's top-level props and methods are private by default — encapsulated. Add scope keywords to the <script> tag to expose them: public (both), public-props, public-methods, and their private-* counterparts. An exclude-<name> attribute carves a single exception out of the currently open group (camelCase names become kebab-case, e.g. getAge → exclude-get-age). Attributes read left to right, so each keyword opens a group that following exclude-* attach to.
sections.code:
<!-- No attributes → everything PRIVATE (the default) -->
<script> … </script>

<!-- Expose all props AND methods -->
<script public> … </script>

<!-- Expose props only; methods stay private -->
<script public-props> … </script>

<!-- Public props EXCEPT one; private methods EXCEPT one -->
<script public-props exclude-state private-methods exclude-get-age> … </script>

sections.heading: Scoped CSS
sections.body: A component's <style> is automatically scoped — selectors only affect that component's own elements, so nothing leaks. Just write normal CSS.
sections.code: Hello

sections.heading: Transitions
sections.body: Wrap a single element in <transition> to animate it as it enters or leaves. The intro plays when the element is added (e.g. by an <if>); its removal is deferred so the outro can finish first. Built in, no import needed: fade, fly, scale, slide, and draw — each takes delay, duration, and a named easing (the full set lives on olum.easings). Write a custom one as a plain function (node, params) => ({ css, tick }) — the same contract Svelte uses.
sections.code:
Fades in and out

Flies in, fades out

sections.heading: Animating lists: flip & crossfade
sections.body: Inside a keyed <for>, <transition> plays its in/out as items are added or removed. Add a flip attribute and a reordered item animates smoothly to its new position (needs a keyed loop, since that's how identity survives the reorder). crossfade() returns a [send, receive] pair — put out="send({ key })" on the element leaving one spot and in="receive({ key })" on the element entering another spot with the same key, and the two fly between them as a single element. Key off a primitive id, not an object — state is deeply reactive, so object-identity checks won't behave as expected.
sections.code:
• {todo.text}
    •

sections.heading: Debugging: <log>
sections.body: <log> compiles to a console.log call that runs on the initial render and again whenever the logged value changes. It renders nothing in the DOM.
sections.code:
<log>{state.user}</log>
<log>{state.a, state.b}</log>  <!-- multiple args pass through -->

sections.heading: Lifecycle: onMount & host
sections.body: Import onMount from olum and pass a callback that runs when the component mounts; return a function from it to run cleanup on unmount (e.g. when an <if> toggles it off or a keyed item is removed). Inside onMount, host refers to this component's own root element — query within it instead of document.querySelector, which could match another instance.
sections.code:
<script>
  import { onMount } from "olum";

  onMount(() => {
    const main = host.querySelector("main");   // host = this component's root
    console.log("mounted");
    return () => console.log("unmounted");     // cleanup on removal
  });
</script>

sections.heading: Escaping & security
sections.body: OlumJS escapes by default to prevent XSS. Every text and attribute {expr} runs through olum.esc. The only way to render raw HTML is the deliberate opt-out — html="expr" or olum.html() — which you should reserve for trusted, pre-sanitized content.

sections.heading: Routing (olum-router)
sections.body: Routing is file-based — the file tree under src/ is the route table, no config needed. A folder is a route only if it has a page.html; a [param] folder becomes a dynamic segment, read with params(). Use to="/path" for client-side navigation (it adds an active class when the URL matches); a plain href does a hard reload. Navigate programmatically with push, replace, back, forward, and go, and read the current path with pathname(). Add a not-found.html at the root of src/ for a global 404.
sections.code:
blog: {slug}

All posts

sections.heading: Global store (olum-store)
sections.body:
A plain object-style store for state that's shared across components or needs to survive route changes. Define it once in a plain .js module with store({ … }); components subscribe just by reading it during render, and re-render when it changes — no providers, no wiring. It has the same deep reactivity as component state, and writes are microtask-batched the same way, so reach for the store because the data is shared, not for performance. Use method shorthand for actions — this is the store inside a method, but an arrow function won't bind it.

Rule of thumb: local-only data is state; one level down is a prop (plus a callback prop to report changes back up); anything shared across components, or that must outlive a route change, belongs in the store. Prop-drilling through a component that never uses the prop is a sign to move that data to the store instead.

sections.heading: Common mistakes
Cheat-sheet
sections.body: The entire template syntax on one screen — bookmark this block.
sections.code:
{state.value} {a > b ? 'x' : 'y'}
{olum.html(state.snippetHtml)}
… … … … {i} {key} slot content → {children} const { title, children } = props(); // INITIAL snapshot, no onMount needed {props().title} // LATEST value, call anywhere {props().children} // LATEST slot content onMount(() => { host.querySelector("main"); }); … … … About import { push, params, pathname } from "olum"; import { store } from "olum"; export const cart = store({ items: [], add(name) { this.items.push(name); } });

sections.heading: Where to go next
sections.body: That's the whole framework, summarized. For the full detail on any topic, read the [docs](/docs); to try it hands-on, open the [Playground](/playground) and edit a live example. When you're ready to build something, the todo-app pattern — state, a list, events, and a conditional empty state — is a great first project.

title: What Is OlumJS, and Why Does It Exist?
description: The honest story behind OlumJS — a tiny framework for building SPAs with nothing but HTML, CSS, and vanilla JavaScript. What it is, why I built it, who it's for, when to use it (and when not to), its core idea, and the ecosystem around it.

sections.heading: The short version
sections.body:
OlumJS is a tiny JavaScript framework that lets you build single-page apps with nothing but the basics — real HTML, CSS, and vanilla JavaScript. A component isn't a special file format; it's an ordinary .html file. no new language to learn, and the whole runtime is about 11 kb for the core.

It exists for one kind of moment: you have an idea and you want to prove it — fast. This is the story of what Olum is, why I built it, and when you should (and shouldn't) reach for it.

sections.heading: First, the name
sections.body: Let's get the awkward part out of the way. "Olum" comes from a Turkish TV series, and — unfortunately — it means "death." Not exactly the friendliest name for a framework. But it stuck, and I've made peace with it. The name is the least interesting thing about the project; what it stands for is what matters.

sections.heading: The VanillaJS developer's platform
sections.body:
That tagline means a lot to me, because I fall back to basics all the time. Why learn every framework and technology out there when the basics I already know — HTML, CSS, vanilla JavaScript — can build the same apps? Frameworks come and go; the basics have been here forever and aren't going anywhere.

What I always wanted was a way to keep writing vanilla and still get the benefits of a modern SPA — components, routing, reactivity — without signing up for a whole framework's world. That thing didn't exist. So I decided to make it exist, and that's how OlumJS started.

sections.heading: How Olum actually started
sections.body:
I started this project about five years ago. I'd tried React, Vue, and Angular, but I always came back to vanilla — it makes me feel unlimited. Every framework has its benefits and its limits, so why box myself in when vanilla never does? Yes, vanilla has the scalability problem, I know. But it never tells you no.

The turning point was 2020, working at an ebook company. I had a team lead I learned a huge amount from — including a set of utility functions he wrote to make our work easier, and a $ helper a lot like jQuery. I started writing my own utils, beginning with that $ function, and kept expanding them.

I was building their [miniapps platform](https://www.kotobee.com/en/miniapps) at the time. One day I watched a miniapp get injected straight into the ebook reader's DOM — fully encapsulated, with its own scoped HTML, CSS, and JS, without touching the reader around it. That's a component — but pure vanilla. So I improved it a little: I figured out a simple router that renders component A at "/" and component B at "/about", wired it to those components, and at some point realized I'd accidentally built a small, fully-vanilla framework.

sections.heading: Why Olum over React, Vue, or Svelte?
sections.body:
Here's my honest answer. Olum is plain text by about 90%. A component is literally an HTML file, so your editor already knows how to work with it — native formatting, native syntax highlighting, no editor extension to install, and no browser devtools extension to keep compatible across Chrome, Firefox, and the rest. It stays out of your way.

I'll also be honest about its scope. Olum was designed for small to medium-sized applications, with simplicity and developer experience as the primary goals rather than trying to solve every use case. The framework closest to Olum is probably Svelte. Funny enough, I only discovered Svelte after I'd already built Olum. If I'd known about it earlier, I might never have started this project at all. Even today, though, some of Svelte's template syntax—especially its conditionals and loops—doesn't feel as natural to me. I wanted templates to look and feel like HTML first, and that's the difference I care about.

sections.heading: It's just HTML — and that's the whole point
sections.body:
I wanted to remind developers of the basics, because the basics are forever and frameworks always change. So Olum stays as close to plain HTML, CSS, and JS as I can keep it. An HTML file can already hold all three languages — so why invent a .olum extension? Use a real .html file. I'm trying to introduce a solution, not add a new problem.

The one rule that falls out of this: is an attribute code or a string? Native HTML already answers it — onclick is code, class is a string. Olum just follows that. Here's a whole component:
sections.code:
<script>
  const state = { count: 0 };
  const inc = () => state.count++;
</script>

<style>
  button { font-weight: 700; }
</style>

<button onclick="inc()">clicked {state.count} times</button>

sections.heading: Who Olum is for
sections.body:
Olum is for anyone — including beginners who've only just learned HTML, CSS, and a bit of JavaScript. Because it's about 90% vanilla, there's almost nothing new to memorize. If you have an idea and want to try it out, Olum is the choice.

Who is it not for, honestly? Anyone building a medium-to-large application or shipping to production. That's simply not what it's built for — and I'd rather say so plainly than have you find out the hard way.

sections.heading: When to use it — and when not to
sections.body:
Reach for Olum on small projects: hackathons, prototypes, experiments, and side projects. It's designed to turn a sudden idea into a small, working app so you can prove the concept quickly. Every developer has a graveyard of side projects, and the small ones are a perfect fit.

When should you not use Olum? For anything from medium-scale up, or anything production-bound, I'd point you to Svelte instead. Use the right tool for the job — for the small, throwaway-fast stuff, that tool is Olum.

sections.heading: The core idea
sections.body:
If there's a single pillar, it's this: if you know HTML, CSS, and a little vanilla JavaScript, you already know Olum. That's the entire reason it exists — you shouldn't have to spend hours learning a new framework when the basics you already have are more than enough to start.

Everything else follows from that. Components are real .html files with scoped styles. You update the UI by mutating a state object. The template stays HTML. And again — it's meant for small apps and prototypes, so the whole thing is tuned to keep you moving, not to scale to millions of nodes.

sections.heading: How it works under the hood
sections.body:
Olum leans on a compiler. The core is deliberately tiny — it builds the tree and handles events, the store, and the hooks. The compiler does the majority of the work: it turns each .html component into a real JavaScript module ahead of time, which is exactly why the runtime can stay so small.

The router is file-system based. It scans your directories for page.html files and builds a route map, handling routing the classic way under the hood. It doesn't cover every case yet, but it works:
sections.code:
src/
  page.html              →  /
  about/
    page.html            →  /about
  blog/
    page.html            →  /blog
    [slug]/
      page.html          →  /blog/:slug

sections.heading: The ecosystem
sections.body:
Olum is a small constellation of pieces, and it's a solo project today:

- Core (olum) — builds the tree and handles events, the store, and hooks. It stays tiny because the compiler carries the load.
- Compiler (olum-compiler) — compiles each .html component into a real JS module. It's the heart of the system and does most of the work.
- Router (olum-router) — file-system routing: directories plus page.html files become your routes. It works today and is still growing.
- Store (olum-store)  — Plain object-style global state. Define it once, import anywhere — components subscribe just by reading it, no providers or wiring.
- DevTool — not fully ready, but usable. It injects straight into your app when you enable it from the UI or the console — no browser extension, no cross-browser compatibility headaches.
- VS Code extension — optional, since a component is just an HTML file. Install it and it adds snippets and highlights the logic bits like <if> and <for>, so you can tell real HTML apart from Olum logic at a glance.
- Playground — the fastest way to try Olum online, with plenty of examples inspired by Svelte's playground.

The project rests on three pillars: olum (core), olum-router, and olum-compiler

sections.heading: Try it in 30 seconds
sections.body: There are two fast ways in. Online, open the [Playground](/playground) and start editing a live example — no install. Locally, scaffold a project with one command:
sections.code:
# scaffold a new project locally
npx create-olum app-name

# or try it online with zero install:
# https://olumjs.top/playground

sections.heading: Where Olum is going
sections.body:
The vision is simple, and a little stubborn: stay focused on the basics, and keep the current vanilla syntax untouched. I might add to it, but I won't change what's already there — which means if you use Olum today and come back in a few years, there'll be no migration guide waiting for you. Your code will still work.


If any of this resonates — if you've ever wanted to just build the thing without adopting a framework's entire universe — give Olum a try. Start with the [Playground](/playground), or read the [complete guide](/blog/olumjs-guide).
